title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 37:12-36 Soon after this, Joseph’s brothers went to pasture their father’s flocks at Shechem. When they had been gone for some time, Jacob said to Joseph, “Your brothers are pasturing the sheep at Shechem. Get ready, and I will send you to them.” “I’m ready to go,” Joseph replied. “Go and see how your brothers and the flocks are getting along,” Jacob said. “Then come back and bring me a report.” So Jacob sent him on his way, and Joseph traveled to Shechem from their home in the valley of Hebron. When he arrived there, a man from the area noticed him wandering around the countryside. “What are you looking for?” he asked. “I’m looking for my brothers,” Joseph replied. “Do you know where they are pasturing their sheep?” “Yes,” the man told him. “They have moved on from here, but I heard them say, ‘Let’s go on to Dothan.’” So Joseph followed his brothers to Dothan and found them there. When Joseph’s brothers saw him coming, they recognized him in the distance. As he approached, they made plans to kill him. “Here comes the dreamer!” they said. “Come on, let’s kill him and throw him into one of these cisterns. We can tell our father, ‘A wild animal has eaten him.’ Then we’ll see what becomes of his dreams!” But when Reuben heard of their scheme, he came to Joseph’s rescue. “Let’s not kill him,” he said. “Why should we shed any blood? Let’s just throw him into this empty cistern here in the wilderness. Then he’ll die without our laying a hand on him.” Reuben was secretly planning to rescue Joseph and return him to his father. So when Joseph arrived, his brothers ripped off the beautiful robe he was wearing. Then they grabbed him and threw him into the cistern. Now the cistern was empty; there was no water in it. Then, just as they were sitting down to eat, they looked up and saw a caravan of camels in the distance coming toward them. It was a group of Ishmaelite traders taking a load of gum, balm, and aromatic resin from Gilead down to Egypt. Judah said to his brothers, “What will we gain by killing our brother? We’d have to cover up the crime. Instead of hurting him, let’s sell him to those Ishmaelite
weekTheme: People may reject us, but God never will. This week has one big idea. Helping you learn how to deal with the difficulty of conflict. The vision this week is to see how conflict is inevitable in life and the consequences it can have in our stories. Joseph's initial place of conflict is going to come from his immediate family. He is going to have to learn to deal with the difficulty of being misunderstood, rejected, betrayed, separated, and lied about from those he loves. Every teenager is going to face these realities. Knowing about them in advance and not being naive about the inevitable relational conflicts of life will give you a real head start on the path to manhood.

days.title: Misunderstood
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:18-24 18 When Joseph’s brothers saw him coming, they recognized him in the distance. As he approached, they made plans to kill him. 19 “Here comes the dreamer!” they said… 23 So when Joseph arrived, his brothers ripped off the beautiful robe he was wearing. 24 Then they grabbed him and threw him into the cistern.
days.reflection: One of the hardest things about being a teenager is the feeling of being misunderstood. It can be hard to communicate what is really happening in our hearts and to learn who to trust with the stuff we are dealing with. And heading into the teenage years, you start feeling all kinds of new stuff. Dreams and temptations both begin to rise. New questions flood in, and sometimes you can feel both embarrassed and even a little guilty about what is happening with some of the stuff going through your head. We can also struggle to communicate clearly with others because we don’t have all the pieces of the puzzle. The dreams we have can sometimes feel a bit vague and uncertain, even if they get us excited. When we do take the risk and share what’s happening inside us, the people we tell can use it against us. They can begin to ridicule, even mock, the stuff we have trusted them with. This is what happened with Joseph and his brothers. When they saw him coming, they took the dream God had given him and used it against him. “Here comes the dreamer,” they said, and they planned to kill not only the dream, but Joseph as well. Can you imagine how painful this must have been for Joseph? How dangerous his dream ended up becoming. His brothers' jealousy and Joseph's lack of discernment created a great distance between them. As you move through life, you will have to learn to deal with the difficulty of being misunderstood. No matter how hard you try, there are just going to be people who don’t get you. They are not going to get the way you think, get what you like, get your perspective, or get the things you do. When they express they don’t understand you, or call you weird or stupid, or mock what you like, it can be really painful. One of the things we really want is friends who can understand where we are coming from. Friends who say, “Yeah, me too” when we tell them what’s happening. But there will be seasons that you have to learn to live in the tension of being misunderstood. Jesus' disciples often misunderstood him. The churches the Apostle Paul started sometimes misunderstood him. All of us will experience this difficulty. The good news you will learn over time is that even though your friends and family may misunderstand you, God always knows what’s going on in your heart and He completely gets you. Psalm 103:13-14 says this, “The LORD is like a father to his children, tender and compassionate to those who fear him. For he knows how weak we are; he remembers we are only dust.” Joseph learned that even if others couldn’t make sense of him, God would, and you are going to learn that too.
questions: Is there a recent moment where you were trying to communicate something to some friends, or something felt important to you, but the people around you didn’t seem to get it? What was it, how did it make you feel? Who is the hardest person to understand that you know? Who do you struggle to communicate with? Have you ever experienced a time at school where other kids picked on you or made fun of you? If you feel comfortable, share what happened.

thoughts.verse: This High Priest of ours understands our weaknesses, for he faced all of the same testings we do, yet he did not sin. So let us come boldly to the throne of our gracious God. There we will receive his mercy, and we will find grace to help us when we need it most.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his love, and ask him to show you even more clearly how deep and real his love is for you.

days.title: Rejection
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:23-24 23 So when Joseph arrived, his brothers ripped off the beautiful robe he was wearing. 24 Then they grabbed him and threw him into the cistern. Now the cistern was empty; there was no water in it.
days.reflection: For Joseph, things have taken a sharp turn for the worse. He has gone from being misunderstood as a dreamer to outright rejection from his brothers - the very ones who are supposed to love him and defend him. Joseph has experienced one of the deepest pains a man will ever face: the pain of rejection. He has gone from being seen as chosen and special and set apart with an amazing robe, to having that ripped from him and being thrown into an empty well. He is experiencing the difficulty of going from a favored son to a rejected brother. He couldn’t fight back. He couldn’t defend himself. There were a lot of them and Joseph was all on his own. Where he expected love and support, he has found rejection and resentment. Learning to deal with the difficulty of rejection requires incredible strength. In fact, in order to be able to deal with it, you are going to need God's help. Rejection can cause a deep and painful sore in our heart. A sore so bad that it's called a wound. A wound is a place in a person’s heart where a relational injury has occurred that still causes pain whenever someone goes near it. We can experience rejection in lots of ways in our lives today. We can try out for spot on a team and get rejected from being a starter. We can apply for a job and be rejected because someone else got it. You can really like a girl, finally get the courage to talk to her and then get rejected by her. As much as these hurt, you are going to need to learn to navigate rejection. To learn how not to base your sense of worth on the opinions of others. To learn to rely on who God says you are, not just what other people think. Jesus will help you with this, because he knows what it's like to be rejected. He came into this world to love and save people, but many rejected him and ultimately crucified him. He wasn’t just stripped of a robe, but stripped of his very life. He wasn’t just thrown into a well, he was nailed to a cross. He knows what it's like and can comfort you and help you to learn to deal with the difficulty of rejection. That’s why Psalm 34:18 says with confidence, “The LORD is close to the brokenhearted; he rescues those whose spirits are crushed.”
questions: Can you think of a scene in one of your favorite movies when the hero had to deal with the difficulty of rejection? What makes this moment so powerful?
Ask the person you’re reading this with to share a time they had to deal with the difficulty of rejection.
Is there a moment when you felt rejected by someone close to you? Would you be willing to share it?

thoughts.verse: He came into the very world he created, but the world didn’t recognize him. He came to his own people and even they rejected him. But to all who believed him and accepted him, he gave the right to become children of God.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Spend a few moments asking God to strengthen your heart and make you secure when others reject you.

days.title: Betrayal
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:26-27 26 Judah said to his brothers, “What will we gain by killing our brother? We’d have to cover up the crime. 27 Instead of hurting him, let’s sell him to those Ishmaelite traders. After all, he is our brother—our own flesh and blood!” And his brothers agreed.
days.reflection: Bad to worse. This is Joseph's life summed up in 3 words. Most brothers can't agree on anything. Which sports team is best; what pizza is best; which one of them is the best. But here we see these brothers agree on one thing. "We should sell Joseph out." They agreed together against Joseph. This agreement wasn’t just about rejection; it was about betrayal. At this moment, something is going to happen to Joseph from which he is never going to be able to return from. Rejection is one thing. It can be worked through. You can deal with conflict, repair relationships, and work to make things right. But betrayal by those who love us is something few come back from. Joseph's brothers are doing something horrific to him. He is being cancelled. Not just symbolically, but actually. They are forcing him out of his family’s story. They are saying to him that they don’t want a life that he is a part of. They are saying that he is going to be worth whatever they can sell him for to whoever happens to be going by. Though this scene in Joseph's life is just a few verses long, can you imagine how deep this pain of betrayal sank into Joseph's heart? When betrayed, it can feel like the end of the world. Where can you go when everyone around you kicks you out of their life? All of a sudden, you have no relationships to help you when you are beat down and sad, no one to tell you things will be ok when you are scared, and no one to guide you into the future. And at some point, if it hasn’t happened already, someone you love who you won’t expect could betray you. The good news is that when we are betrayed by even our closest friends, God will stand by us and comfort us. Jesus knows what it's like to be betrayed. He was betrayed for 30 silver coins by one of his own disciples. He will help you with the sense of rejection and pain if you learn to lean on him. He can share your pain and sorrow and give you strength when you feel like everyone else has bailed. Joseph had to learn this. He had to overcome the difficulty of betrayal. Hebrews 13:5-6 says, “Don’t love money; be satisfied with what you have. For God has said, 'I will never fail you. I will never abandon you.' So we can say with confidence,'The LORD is my helper, so I will have no fear. What can mere people do to me?'" It may feel like people can do a lot to you and like you have lost to fear, but if you calm yourself down and ask God to meet you, you can learn to overcome betrayal through his power, presence and love.
questions: In almost every movie or cartoon, there is a scene where the hero is betrayed by someone close to him. Can you think of a “betrayal” scene? What made this memorable to you?
What do you think is happening in the heart of the person who betrays someone else? How do you think a person’s heart is shaped to do that to someone else?

Betrayal happens way more in people’s lives that you would think. People can betray their spouse in their marriage; kids can betray their parents; parents can betray their kids. Is there anything you can think to do to help strengthen relationships so betrayal is less likely?

thoughts.verse: Even if my father and mother abandon me, the LORD will hold me close.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Spend a few moments thanking Jesus that he understands everything you have been through and will face. He is fiercely loyal to you, and will stick by you when everyone else bails.

days.title: Separation
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:23-28 23 So when Joseph arrived, his brothers ripped off the beautiful robe he was wearing. 24 Then they grabbed him and threw him into the cistern. Now the cistern was empty; there was no water in it. 25 Then, just as they were sitting down to eat, they looked up and saw a caravan of camels in the distance coming toward them. It was a group of Ishmaelite traders taking a load of gum, balm, and aromatic resin from Gilead down to Egypt. 26 Judah said to his brothers, “What will we gain by killing our brother? We’d have to cover up the crime. 27 Instead of hurting him, let’s sell him to those Ishmaelite traders. After all, he is our brother—our own flesh and blood!” And his brothers agreed. 28 So when the Ishmaelites, who were Midianite traders, came by, Joseph’s brothers pulled him out of the cistern and sold him to them for twenty pieces of silver. And the traders took him to Egypt.
days.reflection: Having to move to a new place can be such a painful experience when you are young. Leaving your friends and everything you know, starting again, meet new people, navigating all the different groups of kids and trying to fit in - it can be exhausting and a little scary. Now, imagine you have to move, but this time you are forced to move because you are sold into slavery. You are taken to another city with different customs and culture, and you don’t have any power to stand up for yourself or to gain your freedom to go home. Joseph was just 17 when he found himself a stranger in a strange land. He would have to learn to confront the difficulty of separation. Being away from what we know and love can make us feel really insecure and fearful about our situation. We can face temptations and trials with no one there to check in on us and make sure we are doing the right thing. You are going to have to overcome the difficulty of separation as you grow up. In fact, you have started to do this already. Lots of kids cry on their first day of kindergarten. It's weird to be dumped with other strange kids and no parents. Then, there is separation in middle school. It's further away from your home and there is more pressure from the kids around you. Then, high school, where you have to navigate social groups, avoid bullies, and make friends. This pattern will go on for the rest of your life. You will leave your home and go to college; leave your college and go to a new place to work; leave that job and get married and move to your own home. It just goes on and on. Learning to stand on your own when you are separated from what you know and those you love is a key trait of manhood. It can be terrifying, sometimes even exciting, but it is something heading your way. In fact, God has hard wired and designed us to learn to go into the world and build our own lives. But, as we move out, he will always be with us. Psalms 139:7-12 says, "I can never escape from your Spirit! I can never get away from your presence! If I go up to heaven, you are there; if I go down to the grave, you are there. If I ride the wings of the morning, if I dwell by the farthest oceans, even there your hand will guide me, and your strength will support me. I could ask the darkness to hide me and the light around me to become night but even in darkness I cannot hide from you. To you the night shines as bright as day. Darkness and light are the same to you." Joseph learned to handle separation and distance by relying on God. You will too.
questions: Have you ever had to move to a new home or a new school? Been the “new kid?” What sort of feelings did you feel? If not, have you ever had a new kid move to your school? What did you notice about how they felt or tried to fit in?
Do you remember the first time you were ever away from your family? Maybe at a camp? Or spending time with some relatives? Were there any moments you felt homesick or afraid?
What do you think Joseph could have done to comfort himself in a strange new city, in a strange new house, with strange new people? What would you have done in his situation?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. And I am convinced that nothing can ever separate us from God’s love. Neither death nor life, neither angels nor demons, neither our fears for today nor our worries about tomorrow—not even the powers of hell can separate us from God’s love. No power in the sky above or in the earth below—indeed, nothing in all creation will ever be able to separate us from the love of God that is revealed in Christ Jesus our Lord. Romans 8:38-39 Read through the one you like the most slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you from it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Spend some time thanking God that he will never leave you and forsake you. If you are feeling the challenge of being separated from your support system, ask God to give you comfort, help, and ideas for how to cope.

days.title: Lies
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:29-35 29 Some time later, Reuben returned to get Joseph out of the cistern. When he discovered that Joseph was missing, he tore his clothes in grief. 30 Then he went back to his brothers and lamented, “The boy is gone! What will I do now?” 31 Then the brothers killed a young goat and dipped Joseph’s robe in its blood. 32 They sent the beautiful robe to their father with this message: “Look at what we found. Doesn’t this robe belong to your son?” 33 Their father recognized it immediately. “Yes,” he said, “it is my son’s robe. A wild animal must have eaten him. Joseph has clearly been torn to pieces!” 34 Then Jacob tore his clothes and dressed himself in burlap. He mourned deeply for his son for a long time. 35 His family all tried to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “I will go to my grave mourning for my son,” he would say, and then he would weep.
days.reflection: Lies destroy the world. There isn’t really such a thing as a “white lie.” Small lies turn into bigger lies; bigger lies turn into monster lies. Joseph's brothers have no idea how much pain and trouble their lie is going to cause their father and their future. The hard part for Joseph is that he has no control over his own story. Lies are spreading about him that are impossible for him to respond to. His brothers are going to have to manage the lie they have told. This one lie about Joseph is going to have to be relied again hundreds of times over the years. Covering things up once means they are going to have to cover it up again and again to keep it covered. And this is a big lesson. Lies don’t solve problems; they create problems. Lies don’t fix things; they break things. The lie about Joseph's death caused unbelievable and unnecessary pain to his father's heart. It bonded the brothers together in evil as they had to maintain a united front. It hurt their consciences whenever his death was bought up. It hung over them like a cloud, as we shall see later on in this unit. Lies come from Satan. John 8:44 says, “He has always hated the truth, because there is no truth in him. When he lies, it is consistent with his character; for he is a liar and the father of lies.” Satan is the Father of lies. That means he loves to come up with them and damage people through them. It's what comes out of his mouth, into our minds, and out into the lives of other people. As you go through life, it is inevitable that people are going to lie about you. They are going to say that you said things you didn’t. They will blame you for things you didn’t do to get themselves out of trouble or make themselves look good. As you get older, the lies get larger and have more consequences too. People may lie in the workplace about you causing you to get in trouble with your employer. They may lie to a girl you like because they are jealous or like her too. They may lie on a resume to get a job so you miss out. We have to learn to live in such a way that the lies bounce off of us because we are trustworthy and noble men. We need to entrust our reputation to God and believe that he will honor the truth in the end. Joseph is going to have to deal with people lying about him later in his own story, and you are going to have to as well. Joseph has learned to deal with the difficulty of conflict. He has faced the challenge of being misunderstood, the pain of rejection, the sting of betrayal, the agony of separation, and the hurt of lies. And yet, God has been working behind the scenes in ways Joseph cannot see. And he will work in your life in surprising ways as you face these same things. With God's help, you can overcome the difficulty of conflict.
questions: What are some common lies floating around the culture that you have noticed? Why don’t people seem to be bothered by them even when they know they may not be true?
Has someone ever lied about you? What was the lie, and how did it impact your life?

There was a courageous Russian man named Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn who lived in a time where everyone was lying. He was put into prison for the truth and went through immense suffering to hold to what was true. When he got out of prison and became somewhat of a hero, he said this: "One word of truth outweighs the world.” What do you think he might have meant by that?

thoughts.verse: For the Scriptures say, 'If you want to enjoy life and see many happy days, keep your tongue from speaking evil and your lips from telling lies.'
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Ask God to make you a man of the truth, and to learn to resist lies in any form. Ask him for help if you are dealing with a situation where someone has been lying about you.

extras: WEEK 2 MANSCHOOL CONFLICT AND CHOCOLATE This time is a chance for you to share some of your own story with your son. It’s a chance for you to sharpen your story sharing skills as well. Go through the 5 ideas from this week around the difficulties of conflict: Being misunderstood. Being rejected. Being betrayed. Being separated from your family. Being lied about. Share what happened. Share how it made you feel. Share what the consequences were. Share how you handled it. The goal of this time is to let your son know you have had to learn to deal with the difficulty of conflict, and you want to be there to help walk him through it when he faces it. It's also time to have fun. Get a bunch of different foods and cover them in chocolate. The more bizarre and varied, the better. Start safe and work it up. (You may need to google what chocolate to buy and how to get it to melt without burning. Also, tell your son that by learning this. he may be able to make chocolate covered strawberries for his girl one day.) Chocolate covered Oreos. Chocolate covered potato chips. Chocolate covered fruit. Chocolate covered Slim Jims. Chocolate covered bacon. Chocolate covered pickles. Chocolate covered Cheetos. Chocolate covered Altoids. Then, get him to rate it from favorite to most disgusting. Offer some to the other folks in your family.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
